description: Fever in children - body temperature above 100.4F (38C)
severity_questions: How many days has the fever lasted?
What is the approximate temperature?
Is there any rash, vomiting, or seizures?
action_mild: Give paracetamol syrup (10-15mg per kg body weight, every 6 hours). Keep child hydrated with ORS or clean water. Sponge with lukewarm (not cold) water. Light clothing.
action_severe: Go to nearest hospital immediately if: fever lasts more than 3 days, child has seizures or convulsions, child refuses to drink, rash appears, child is unusually drowsy.
disclaimer: This is general first-aid guidance only. Please consult a doctor for proper diagnosis and treatment.

description: Diarrhea - frequent loose or watery stools, possibly with vomiting
severity_questions: How many loose stools in the last 24 hours?
Is there blood in the stool?
Is the person able to drink fluids?
action_mild: Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) after every loose stool. If ORS not available, mix 6 teaspoons sugar and half teaspoon salt in 1 litre clean water. Continue feeding. Zinc tablets for children (20mg/day for 10-14 days).
action_severe: Go to hospital immediately if: blood in stool, unable to drink, sunken eyes, very dry mouth, no urination for 6+ hours, high fever with diarrhea.
disclaimer: This is general first-aid guidance only. Please consult a doctor for proper diagnosis and treatment.

description: Snake bite - requires immediate medical attention
severity_questions: How long ago did the bite happen?
Did you see the snake? What did it look like?
Is there swelling or pain at the bite site?
action_mild: Keep the person calm and still. Remove jewelry near bite site. Keep bitten limb below heart level. Do NOT cut the wound, suck venom, or apply tourniquet. Do NOT apply ice. Mark the edge of swelling with pen and time.
action_severe: ALL snake bites need hospital treatment. Call ambulance (108) immediately. Anti-venom must be given within 2-4 hours. Go to nearest government hospital - they have free anti-venom.
disclaimer: Snake bites are medical emergencies. Go to hospital immediately regardless of symptoms.

description: Burns from fire, hot water, hot oil, or chemicals
severity_questions: What caused the burn (fire, hot water, chemicals)?
How large is the burned area?
Are there blisters or is the skin charred?
action_mild: Cool the burn under clean running water for 10-20 minutes. Do NOT apply ice, butter, toothpaste, or oil. Cover with clean cloth. Give paracetamol for pain.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: burn is larger than palm size, burn is on face/hands/joints, burn has charred skin, chemical burn, electrical burn, burn on child under 5.
disclaimer: This is general first-aid guidance only. Please consult a doctor for proper diagnosis and treatment.

description: Dehydration from heat, diarrhea, or not drinking enough water
severity_questions: When did the person last urinate?
Is the person dizzy or confused?
Has there been vomiting or diarrhea?
action_mild: Give ORS solution or clean water with salt and sugar. Rest in shade. Small sips frequently (not large amounts at once). If due to heat, cool the body with wet cloth.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: no urination for 8+ hours, person is confused or unconscious, sunken eyes, skin does not bounce back when pinched, rapid heartbeat.
disclaimer: This is general first-aid guidance only. Please consult a doctor for proper diagnosis and treatment.

description: Warning signs during pregnancy that need immediate medical attention
severity_questions: How many months pregnant?
Is there any bleeding?
Is there severe headache or blurred vision?
action_mild: Regular prenatal checkups are essential. Take iron and folic acid tablets daily. Eat nutritious food. Rest adequately. Visit ASHA worker for free check-ups.
action_severe: Go to hospital IMMEDIATELY if: vaginal bleeding, severe headache, blurred vision, swelling of face/hands, baby not moving, water breaks early, high fever, convulsions.
disclaimer: Pregnancy complications are medical emergencies. Always consult a doctor. Call 108 for ambulance.

description: Dog or animal bite - risk of rabies
severity_questions: How long ago did the bite happen?
Was it a stray dog or a pet?
Is the wound bleeding?
action_mild: Wash wound thoroughly with soap and running water for 15 minutes. Apply antiseptic. Do NOT apply turmeric, chili, or other home remedies on wound.
action_severe: ALL dog/animal bites need anti-rabies vaccination. Go to government hospital immediately - vaccine is FREE. Rabies is 100% fatal if untreated. Complete the full course of 4-5 injections.
disclaimer: Animal bites require medical treatment for rabies prevention. Go to hospital immediately.

description: Heat stroke or heat exhaustion from extreme heat exposure
severity_questions: Is the person sweating or has sweating stopped?
Is the person confused or unconscious?
What is the person's temperature?
action_mild: Move to shade immediately. Remove excess clothing. Pour cool water on body. Fan the person. Give ORS or cool water to drink. Place wet cloth on neck, armpits, groin.
action_severe: Call 108 immediately if: person is unconscious, not sweating despite heat, temperature above 104F (40C), seizures, confusion. Heat stroke can be fatal.
disclaimer: This is general first-aid guidance only. Please consult a doctor for proper diagnosis and treatment.

description: Cuts, wounds, and bleeding injuries
severity_questions: How deep is the cut?
Is the bleeding stopping with pressure?
When was the last tetanus injection?
action_mild: Clean wound with clean water. Apply firm pressure with clean cloth for 10 minutes. Apply antiseptic. Cover with clean bandage. Keep wound clean and dry.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: bleeding does not stop after 15 minutes of pressure, wound is deep or gaping, wound from rusty object (need tetanus shot), wound on face, signs of infection (redness, pus, fever).
disclaimer: This is general first-aid guidance only. Please consult a doctor for proper diagnosis and treatment.

description: Chest pain - could be heart attack or other causes
severity_questions: Where exactly is the pain?
Does the pain spread to arm, jaw, or back?
Is there sweating, nausea, or shortness of breath?
action_mild: If mild and comes with coughing or after eating, it may be acidity. Rest and observe. Take antacid if needed.
action_severe: Call 108 IMMEDIATELY if: crushing pain in center of chest, pain spreading to left arm/jaw/back, sweating, nausea, shortness of breath, dizziness. Chew one aspirin tablet (300mg) while waiting for ambulance. Do NOT drive yourself.
disclaimer: Chest pain can be a heart attack. Call 108 immediately. Do not delay.

description: Food poisoning from contaminated food or water
severity_questions: When did you eat the suspected food?
How many times have you vomited?
Is there diarrhea along with vomiting?
action_mild: Sip ORS or clean water slowly. Do not eat solid food for a few hours. Once vomiting stops, start with bland food (khichdi, dal pani). Rest.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: blood in vomit or stool, high fever, unable to keep any fluids down for 6+ hours, severe stomach cramps, symptoms in a pregnant woman or child under 5.
disclaimer: This is general first-aid guidance only. Please consult a doctor for proper diagnosis and treatment.

description: Difficulty breathing or shortness of breath
severity_questions: How suddenly did it start?
Is there chest pain or tightness?
Does the person have asthma or any lung condition?
action_mild: Help person sit upright (do not lie down). Loosen tight clothing. Move to fresh air. If known asthmatic, help use their inhaler. Stay calm.
action_severe: Call 108 IMMEDIATELY if: lips or fingernails turning blue, unable to speak in full sentences, gasping, wheezing getting worse, choking on object, breathing difficulty after allergic reaction.
disclaimer: Breathing difficulty can be life-threatening. Call 108 immediately if severe.

description: Accidental or intentional poisoning from chemicals, pesticides, or medicines
severity_questions: What was consumed and how much?
How long ago?
Is the person conscious and breathing?
action_mild: Do NOT induce vomiting (it can cause more damage). Keep person calm. If poison is on skin, remove clothing and wash with water. Save the poison container/label for doctors.
action_severe: Call 108 IMMEDIATELY. ALL poisoning cases need hospital treatment. Bring the poison container to hospital. If person is unconscious, place in recovery position (on side). Do not give anything by mouth to unconscious person.
disclaimer: Poisoning is a medical emergency. Go to hospital immediately. National Poison Information Centre: 011-26593677.

description: Suspected bone fracture from fall, accident, or injury
severity_questions: How did the injury happen?
Can you move the injured part?
Is there visible deformity or bone sticking out?
action_mild: Do NOT try to straighten the limb. Immobilize the injured area with a splint (use stick, cardboard). Apply ice wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling. Give paracetamol for pain.
action_severe: Go to hospital for all suspected fractures. Call 108 if: bone is visible through skin (open fracture), injury is to head/neck/spine, person cannot feel or move below injury, heavy bleeding with fracture.
disclaimer: All suspected fractures need X-ray and medical treatment. Do not delay hospital visit.

description: Eye injury from foreign object, chemical splash, or trauma
severity_questions: What caused the eye injury?
Is there any chemical involved?
Can you see clearly from the affected eye?
action_mild: For small particle: flush eye with clean water for 10 minutes. Blink repeatedly. Do NOT rub the eye. If particle visible, try to remove with clean wet cloth corner.
action_severe: Go to hospital IMMEDIATELY if: chemical splash (flush with water for 20 minutes first), sharp object injury, sudden vision loss, severe pain, bleeding from eye. Do NOT try to remove embedded objects.
disclaimer: Eye injuries can cause permanent vision loss. See a doctor promptly.

description: Dengue fever - transmitted by Aedes mosquito. High fever, severe headache, joint and muscle pain, rash. Can become severe dengue with bleeding and plasma leakage.
severity_questions: How high is the fever?
Is there any bleeding from nose or gums?
Are there red spots on skin?
Is the person vomiting blood or passing blood in stool?
action_mild: Rest in shade. Drink plenty of fluids - ORS, coconut water, fruit juices. Take paracetamol for fever (NOT ibuprofen/aspirin - they increase bleeding risk). Do not take antibiotics. Monitor platelet count if possible.
action_severe: Go to hospital IMMEDIATELY if: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding from gums/nose, blood in stool/vomit, difficulty breathing, confusion, platelet count dropping rapidly. Severe dengue can be fatal within hours. Give ORS while shifting to hospital.
disclaimer: Dengue can be fatal. Do not ignore warning signs. Hospital care is essential for severe cases.

description: Malaria - transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito. Fever with chills, headache, muscle pain, sweating. Common in monsoon and post-monsoon in rural areas.
severity_questions: How many days of fever?
Is there shaking with cold?
Have you traveled to a malaria-prone area recently?
Is there confusion or drowsiness?
action_mild: Go to health center for blood test (RDT/microscopy). If positive, take full course of ACT (Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy) - it is FREE at government health centers. Rest, drink fluids. Complete full treatment even if feeling better.
action_severe: Go to hospital immediately if: confusion, seizures, severe anemia (pale gums, weakness), unable to drink, passing dark urine, jaundice (yellow eyes/skin). Falciparum malaria can be deadly within 24 hours.
disclaimer: Malaria requires proper medical treatment with prescribed medicines. Do not self-medicate. Free ACT medicines available at all government health centers.

description: Typhoid fever - caused by Salmonella bacteria, spread through contaminated food/water. High fever (104F+), stomach pain, weakness, headache. Common in monsoon.
severity_questions: How many days of continuous fever?
Is there severe stomach pain?
Is there constipation or persistent diarrhea?
Is the person confused?
action_mild: Blood test (Widal test or Typhi dot) needed for diagnosis. Take full course of antibiotics as prescribed (usually azithromycin or ceftriaxone). Eat light food. Drink clean water. Rest for 2 weeks to avoid complications.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: high fever lasting more than 5 days, severe abdominal pain, intestinal perforation symptoms (severe pain, rigid abdomen), confusion, jaundice, or if person is a child or pregnant woman. Surgery may be needed for intestinal perforation.
disclaimer: Typhoid requires antibiotics. Do not ignore prolonged fever. Vaccination available (Typhoid conjugate vaccine - one dose for children above 6 months).

description: Severe toothache with swelling or infection in mouth
severity_questions: Is there swelling of face or jaw?
Is there fever with the tooth pain?
Can you open mouth fully?
Is there pus discharge?
action_mild: Rinse mouth with warm salt water. Use ice pack outside cheek for 10 minutes. Take ibuprofen or paracetamol for pain. Do NOT put aspirin directly on gum (burns tissue). See dentist as soon as possible.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: swelling spreading to eye/neck/chest (can block breathing), fever with chills, difficulty swallowing or opening mouth, pus discharge. Infection can spread to jaw and cause serious complications.
disclaimer: Dental infections can spread to bloodstream. Do not ignore spreading swelling.

description: Epileptic seizure or convulsion - uncontrolled body movement, stiffening, frothing from mouth, loss of consciousness
severity_questions: How long did the seizure last?
Is the person breathing normally now?
Is this the first seizure or known epilepsy?
Did the person injure their head?
action_mild: For a person having seizure: Do NOT hold them down. Do NOT put anything in mouth. Turn them on side. Clear area of hard/sharp objects. Time the seizure. After seizure stops, keep them calm and let them rest.
action_severe: Call 108 immediately if: seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, person doesn't wake up between seizures, first-time seizure, seizure with high fever, person is pregnant, breathing stops. Do NOT leave person alone.
disclaimer: Epilepsy is manageable with proper medication. Do not hide epilepsy from doctor. Free medicines available under National Epilepsy Control Programme.

description: Asthma attack - severe wheezing, coughing, chest tightness, difficulty breathing. May be triggered by dust, pollen, cold air, exercise.
severity_questions: Is the person able to speak full sentences?
Are lips or fingernails blue?
Did they use their inhaler? How many times?
Is there confusion or drowsiness?
action_mild: Help person sit upright. Loosen tight clothing. If they have asthma inhaler (blue pump), help them use it - one puff, wait 1 minute, can repeat up to 4 times. Keep them calm, do not panic.
action_severe: Call 108 IMMEDIATELY if: inhaler not helping after 4 puffs, lips/fingernails blue, cannot speak at all, rapid breathing, confusion, collapse. Asthma attack can be fatal within minutes.
disclaimer: Asthma requires daily controller medication (usually inhaled steroid). Spacer device makes inhaler more effective. Free medicines available at government health centers.

description: Jaundice - yellowing of eyes and skin, dark urine, pale stool, weakness. Caused by liver problems (hepatitis, bile duct issues). Very common in rural areas due to contaminated water.
severity_questions: How many days of yellow eyes/skin?
Is there high fever with it?
Is the person confused or drowsy?
Is there swelling of legs or abdomen?
action_mild: Rest completely. Eat light diet - khichdi, dal, fruits, vegetables. Avoid fatty food, oil, fried items, alcohol. Drink clean boiled water. Visit doctor for liver function tests.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: high fever with jaundice, severe weakness, confusion, swelling in legs/abdomen, vomiting blood, passing black stool. Could be severe hepatitis or liver failure.
disclaimer: Jaundice needs medical evaluation. Do not ignore yellow eyes/skin. Hepatitis A and E spread through contaminated food/water - maintain hygiene.

description: Low blood pressure causing dizziness, weakness, fainting
severity_questions: Is the person able to stand?
Is there chest pain or breathlessness?
Did they lose consciousness?
Are they vomiting?
action_mild: Help person lie down and raise legs slightly. Give ORS water or salted water to drink. If person is diabetic, check blood sugar. Let them rest until stable. If on BP medicines, consult doctor about dose.
action_severe: Go to hospital if: person fainted and did not regain consciousness quickly, chest pain, breathlessness, confusion, seizure, signs of internal bleeding (black stool, vomiting blood). Low BP can be due to heart problems, bleeding, or severe infection.
disclaimer: Recurrent low BP needs medical investigation. Do not stop BP medicines without doctor advice.
